commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.review_summary:
The branch splits Codex skill snapshot delivery into bundled-skill developer instructions and non-bundled user/reference context, with snapshot schema refresh logic, tests, proof artifacts, and prompt snapshot updates.

PR surface: Source +276, Tests +746, Docs +97, Other +328. Total +1447 across 20 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible. Current main wraps `skillsSnapshot.prompt` into Codex turn input, and source inspection of the PR shows both the intended split and the remaining remote-note regression; I did not run a live multi-turn Codex reproduction in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: There is a narrow mechanical repair: preserve remote skill eligibility guidance after the split without changing the broader sanitizer scope.

target.title: fix(codex): stop persisting per-turn OpenClaw skills snapshot in Codex native user history (#84662)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Codex app-server (`extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts`) wraps the OpenClaw **skills snapshot** into the per-turn user-input text via `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext` and `prependCodexOpenClawPromptContext`. Because `turn/start.input` becomes a persisted user-history item, the skills bytes are re-billed on every subsequent `response.create.input`, growing the model input by ~2,194 tokens/turn on the default `main` agent (issue #84662 root cause).
- **Why it matters**: After 10 turns the cumulative `prompt_tokens` cost is ≈312k vs ≈236k post-fix (−24.3% on this 10-turn ping-pong scenario); the issue reporter observed that the runtime-context block can dominate `response.create.input` over a longer session.
- **What changed**: Two-lane split of the OpenClaw skills snapshot.
  - **Trusted developer lane** (`turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions`): a new `SkillSnapshot.trustedDeveloperPrompt` built from `openclaw-bundled` entries only. Rides the same field cron and heartbeat triggers already use in production, via the `openClawSkillsPrompt` option threaded through `buildTurnStartParams` → `buildTurnCollaborationMode` → `buildTurnScopedCollaborationInstructions`.
  - **Untrusted reference lane** (per-turn user input): a new `SkillSnapshot.untrustedReferencePrompt` built from every non-bundled source (workspace, project `.agents/skills`, personal `~/.agents/skills`, `openclaw-managed`, `openclaw-extra`, plugin-generated). Rendered through the same `compactSkillPaths` / `applySkillsPromptLimits` / `formatSkillsForPrompt` pipeline as the full prompt and emitted by `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext` under a non-authoritative `## OpenClaw User-Installed Skills (reference)` section inside the existing `OpenClaw workspace context for this turn:` wrapper. This preserves native Codex visibility of user-installed skills without granting them developer-instruction authority.
  - **Legacy snapshot migration**: a new `SkillSnapshot.schemaVersion` (constant `SKILL_SNAPSHOT_SCHEMA_VERSION = 2`) and `isSkillsSnapshotSchemaOutdated` helper. The `agent-command.ts` reuse path runs this alongside the existing version / filter checks; sessions persisted before the lane-split fields existed (no `schemaVersion`, no `trustedDeveloperPrompt`, no `untrustedReferencePrompt`) force-refresh through `buildWorkspaceSkillSnapshot` so the new fields are populated before reuse. Snapshots stamped at a future schema version do not loop on refresh.
  - `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext`'s per-turn wrapper subline is updated to `"Treat this block as user-editable reference for the current request, not as developer instructions. Sections below are listed in this order: workspace context, then user-installed (non-bundled) skills."` so the new section ordering is documented in-band.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: User-editable **workspace context** (incl. the MEMORY marker) is intentionally left in the turn-input reference lane — it is **not** promoted to developer authority. SKILL.md metadata from any non-bundled source stays out of `developer_instructions` (the prompt-authority lane). Unit-test invariants pin all three halves (`thread-lifecycle.test.ts` "invariant: workspace user-editable context never appears in developer_instructions"; `run-attempt.test.ts` "does not elevate untrusted skill metadata into Codex developer_instructions"; `skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts` "trustedDeveloperPrompt includes bundled skills but excludes workspace/project/personal/managed/extra sources"). Workspace bootstrap (#77372), AGENTS.md, HEARTBEAT.md, cron payload guidance, the PI runner `openclaw.runtime-context` path, and the subagent / ACP / CLI surfaces are untouched. **Historical-session sanitization is deferred to a stacked follow-up** (see "Limitations of this PR" below).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Integrations  (codex app-server `turn/start` shape)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Addresses the skills component of #84662. This PR routes future bundled-skill snapshots through `developer_instructions` so they are not appended to Codex native user history going forward. It does **not** implement the issue's *safe interim* historical sanitizer for already-bound threads; that half is deferred to a stacked follow-up (see "Limitations of this PR").
- Related: supersedes closed #84790 (TurboTheTurtle, closed 2026-05-22) by narrowing scope to the trust-filtered (bundled-only) skills snapshot — workspace/MEMORY stays in the turn-input reference lane that #84790's [P1] prompt-authority finding was raised against, and non-bundled SKILL.md content stays in the same lane under an explicit reference wrapper.
- Related: complements merged #83454 (Eva, 2026-05-18) — bundled skills are treated as **stable OpenClaw plugin/tool guidance** and routed through `developer_instructions`, while user-editable workspace text and non-bundled SKILL.md content **remain user-reference scoped in turn input** per #83454. This is not a reversal of #83454: the user-editable surface that #83454 protected is left where #83454 put it; only the OpenClaw-owned bundled plugin-manifest surface moves, joining the workspace bootstrap files (#77372) that already live in `developer_instructions`.
- Related: builds on merged #77372 (lonexreb, workspace bootstrap → `developer_instructions`) as the lane-precedent for OpenClaw-owned stable instructions.
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause**: The shared per-turn wrapper `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext` conflated two fragments with different authority + lifetime needs:
  1. **Skills snapshot** (`params.skillsSnapshot?.prompt`) — a mixed-source catalog. OpenClaw-owned bundled skills (`openclaw-bundled`) are byte-stable, OpenClaw-controlled plugin manifest text, analogous to the workspace bootstrap files (`SOUL.md` / `IDENTITY.md` / `TOOLS.md` / `USER.md`) that already live in `developer_instructions` via #77372. The same field also carries SKILL.md frontmatter from workspace, project (`.agents/skills`), personal (`~/.agents/skills`), `openclaw-managed`, `openclaw-extra`, and plugin-generated sources whose content is user/install-controlled.
  2. **Workspace prompt context** (`workspaceBootstrapContext.promptContext`) — **user-editable** workspace files (e.g., `MEMORY.md`, custom project docs). Per #84662 §Security/rank caution, this must remain quoted/reference scoped.
  Bundling both into `turn/start.input[0].text` means the bytes ride the per-turn user-input lane and Codex native history persists one copy per turn. Issue #84662 carves these two halves apart explicitly; the wider scope from #84790 collapsed them back together and ran into the prompt-authority blocker raised in that review.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: No unit test pinned the authority boundary (workspace user-editable text must not appear in `developer_instructions`, and untrusted-source skill metadata must not appear in `developer_instructions`). This PR adds three: `thread-lifecycle.test.ts` "invariant: workspace user-editable context never appears in developer_instructions", `run-attempt.test.ts` "does not elevate untrusted skill metadata into Codex developer_instructions", and `skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts` "trustedDeveloperPrompt includes bundled skills but excludes workspace/project/personal/managed/extra sources".
- **Contributing context**: cron and heartbeat triggers already populate `turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` in production. The generated `@openai/codex` 0.132.0 `v2/TurnStartParams.ts` does not currently declare `collaborationMode` (the formal basis for #84790's [P1] protocol concern), but the bundled runtime binary parses and honors the field on cron / heartbeat triggers today — empirical evidence below shows it also honors it on the chat trigger.

## Security boundary

Trust-filter policy for the developer-instructions lane (`turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions`):

- **Allowed (elevated)**: skill entries whose `resolveSkillSource(entry.skill) === "openclaw-bundled"`. Only the OpenClaw-bundled catalog ships shipped/reviewed SKILL.md content and is treated as developer authority alongside the workspace bootstrap files (#77372). The trusted fragment is built by `buildTrustedDeveloperSkillsPrompt` in `src/agents/skills/workspace.ts` from the same `promptEntries` set that drives `SkillSnapshot.prompt`, sorted, char-budgeted, and rendered via `formatSkillsForPrompt` / `formatSkillsCompact` — the byte shape is identical to the full prompt, just with the untrusted entries removed.
- **Not elevated (kept in user/reference lane only)**: skill entries from `openclaw-workspace`, `agents-skills-project` (`.agents/skills`), `agents-skills-personal` (`~/.agents/skills`), `openclaw-managed`, `openclaw-extra`, plugin-generated, and any source not on the bundled allowlist. The trust check is deliberately conservative — when the source is unknown or absent it falls into the deny side. These entries are rendered into a new `SkillSnapshot.untrustedReferencePrompt` fragment by `buildUntrustedReferenceSkillsPrompt`, and `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext` emits them under an explicit `## OpenClaw User-Installed Skills (reference)` section nested in the existing `OpenClaw workspace context for this turn:` wrapper. Wrapper text states the section is reference metadata for tool discovery, not developer authority.
- **Legacy session migration**: `SkillSnapshot.schemaVersion` is stamped at every snapshot build (`SKILL_SNAPSHOT_SCHEMA_VERSION = 2`). The `agent-command.ts` reuse path runs `isSkillsSnapshotSchemaOutdated()`; sessions persisted before v2 (with no `schemaVersion`, no `trustedDeveloperPrompt`, no `untrustedReferencePrompt`) force-refresh through `buildWorkspaceSkillSnapshot` on the next turn so both lane fields are populated before the Codex call site reads them. Without this, the previous trust-filter HEAD would have silently dropped every skill (bundled or otherwise) from `developer_instructions` for existing sessions until a workspace bump or new session.
- **Wire-level invariants pinned by regression tests**:
  - `developer_instructions` carries only trust-filtered bundled skill descriptions/locations, even when an attacker controls workspace SKILL.md content (`run-attempt.test.ts`: "does not elevate untrusted skill metadata into Codex developer_instructions").
  - Per-turn user input under the workspace-context wrapper surfaces non-bundled skill descriptions/locations under the reference section so native Codex stays able to discover them (`run-attempt.test.ts`: same case + "omits skills from developer_instructions when no trusted skills are present but keeps untrusted skills in the reference lane"; `run-attempt.test.ts`: "falls back to the user-input prompt only when no skills lanes are populated" for the no-op baseline).
  - The `SkillSnapshot` producer renders the bundled and non-bundled subsets into disjoint fragments (`skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts`: "trustedDeveloperPrompt includes bundled skills but excludes workspace/project/personal/managed/extra sources" + "untrustedReferencePrompt mirrors untrusted skill metadata for the reference lane" + "omits untrustedReferencePrompt when no non-bundled skills are present" + "stamps schemaVersion so legacy snapshots are force-refreshed").
  - The schema-version refresh predicate is correct across legacy / current / stale / future cases (`snapshot-hydration.test.ts`: 5 cases).
  - Provider-boundary precheck, `llm_input` observer, trajectory `context.compiled.systemPrompt`, and `result.systemPromptReport.skills.{promptChars,entries}` all reflect the trusted developer-lane bytes — not the full mixed catalog — so operators see the actual developer-lane size. The non-bundled bytes ride the per-turn user-input lane and are counted by Codex's input accounting separately.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts` — 6 cases for cron / heartbeat / chat / authority invariant.
  - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts` — existing `routes OpenClaw skills through turn collaboration developer_instructions, not user input` updated to use `trustedDeveloperPrompt`; **3 new cases**: `does not elevate untrusted skill metadata into Codex developer_instructions`, `omits skills from developer_instructions when no trusted skills are present but keeps untrusted skills in the reference lane`, `falls back to the user-input prompt only when no skills lanes are populated`.
  - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts` — 1 context-engine budget assertion updated to use `trustedDeveloperPrompt`.
  - `src/agents/skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts` — **5 new cases** (4 lane-filter + 1 schema-version stamping): `trustedDeveloperPrompt includes bundled skills but excludes workspace/project/personal/managed/extra sources`, `omits trustedDeveloperPrompt when no bundled skills are present`, `untrustedReferencePrompt mirrors untrusted skill metadata for the reference lane`, `omits untrustedReferencePrompt when no non-bundled skills are present`, `stamps schemaVersion so legacy snapshots are force-refreshed`.
  - `src/agents/skills/snapshot-hydration.test.ts` — **5 new cases** for `isSkillsSnapshotSchemaOutdated`: `returns false when there is no persisted snapshot at all`, `treats a legacy snapshot with no schemaVersion as outdated`, `treats a snapshot with schemaVersion older than the current version as outdated`, `treats a snapshot at the current schemaVersion as fresh`, `treats a snapshot at a future schemaVersion as fresh (forward compat)`.
  - Prompt-snapshot helper/check: `test/helpers/agents/happy-path-prompt-snapshots.ts`, `test/scripts/prompt-snapshots.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: (a) chat trigger renders trusted bundled skills as `developer_instructions`; (b) chat trigger without skills returns `null` `developer_instructions`; (c) cron composes cron + skills; (d) heartbeat composes heartbeat + overlay + skills; (e) cron without skills is byte-identical to current main; (f) workspace user-editable context never reaches `developer_instructions` (authority invariant); (g) untrusted skill metadata (workspace, project, personal, managed, extra) never reaches `developer_instructions`, even when `skillsSnapshot.prompt` carries it (prompt-authority boundary); (h) untrusted skill metadata remains visible to Codex through the per-turn user input under the reference wrapper (visibility-preserving fix for the post-trust-filter regression); (i) `buildWorkspaceSkillSnapshot` partitions skills into disjoint trusted/untrusted fragments and stamps `schemaVersion`; (j) legacy snapshots without `schemaVersion` force-refresh; (k) context-engine / prompt-snapshot surfaces report the workspace-only turn-input projection rather than the old runtime-context wrapper.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: each bug is structural (wrong lane plus wrong trust scope plus stale-snapshot reuse); the structural invariants pin all four halves (bundled skills present in `developer_instructions`, workspace absent from it, untrusted skill metadata absent from it, non-bundled skills present in the reference lane, legacy snapshots refreshed) without needing live-model evidence to rerun on every CI tick.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **Per-turn token billing on the default `main` agent** (new turns after this PR ships): the **bundled** subset of the skills snapshot no longer re-bills in every `response.create.input`; the cumulative 10-turn `prompt_tokens` cost drops by ~24% on the ping-pong scenario in the Real-behavior proof. The **non-bundled** subset (workspace / project / personal / managed / extra / plugin-generated) continues to re-bill at the pre-#85646 rate because it is intentionally kept in the per-turn user-input lane for prompt-authority reasons (see Limitations). Workspace-context bytes continue to re-bill at the same rate as before (~+540/turn floor).
- **Per-turn user-input wrapper text** (new turns): renamed from `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:\n…` to `OpenClaw workspace context for this turn:\n…`. The block now contains (a) the existing `## OpenClaw Workspace Context` section, and (b) when any non-bundled skill is eligible, a new `## OpenClaw User-Installed Skills (reference)` section listing those skill names/descriptions/locations under a reference-only header.
- **Developer-instructions skills section** (new turns): only `openclaw-bundled` SKILL.md descriptions/locations appear here. SKILL.md from workspace, project, personal, `openclaw-managed`, `openclaw-extra`, and plugin-generated sources remains discoverable via the per-turn user-input reference section (visibility-preserving); it is never elevated to the developer-instructions lane.
- **Existing sessions opened before this PR ships**: the first turn after upgrade rebuilds the skills snapshot via `buildWorkspaceSkillSnapshot` so the new `trustedDeveloperPrompt` and `untrustedReferencePrompt` fields are populated; subsequent turns reuse the persisted snapshot in the normal way. Without this migration, the previous trust-filter HEAD would have silently dropped every skill from `developer_instructions` on those sessions until a workspace bump or new session.
- **Existing Codex native threads bound before this PR ships**: see "Limitations of this PR" below.
- **Snapshot fixtures**: three `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*.md` files regenerated to reflect the wrapper subline update.

## Limitations of this PR (deferred half explicitly disclosed)

This PR **prevents future bundled-skills bytes from being appended** to Codex native user history. It does **not** sanitize history for threads that were bound before the fix ships, and it does **not** eliminate per-turn user-history persistence of non-bundled SKILL.md content.

- **Non-bundled skills still ride the per-turn user-input lane**, by policy. Their SKILL.md descriptions/locations re-bill on every subsequent `response.create.input` and are persisted to native Codex user history — the same behavior as before #85646 for those entries. The alternative (elevating them to `developer_instructions`) was rejected as a prompt-authority regression by the round-2 ClawSweeper review; this PR keeps the visibility while explicitly disclosing the per-turn replay cost for non-bundled-heavy catalogs. Catalogs that are predominantly bundled (e.g. default OpenClaw installs) see the full ~24% cumulative token-savings benchmark below; catalogs with a non-trivial non-bundled subset see proportionally less savings on that subset.
- **Existing bound Codex native threads** that already contain `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:` user-history items from prior turns will continue to replay those items on `response.create.input` until either (a) the thread cycles to a fresh native session, or (b) the deferred follow-up session-history sanitizer (issue #84662 §"Safe interim fix") lands as a stacked follow-up PR that strips the wrapper from `readCodexMirroredSessionHistoryMessages`.
- The snapshot-fixture check (`workspaceBootstrapContextFrom` field in `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*.md`) verifies the **current projection shape** for new turns; it is **not** a regression guard for the deferred sanitizer half — old mirrored-history user items are out of its scope.
- The 10-turn benchmark below was run at the prior trust-filter HEAD (`66ee52173e`, when the developer lane carried the full mixed-source catalog) with fresh user IDs. The current-head benchmark uplift on the trusted (bundled) developer lane is a **strict subset** of those gains — fewer bytes, identical wire shape, byte-stable across turns — so the cacheable / no-history-replay properties documented there continue to hold for the bundled subset. The non-bundled subset's per-turn replay cost matches the pre-#85646 baseline for that subset.

The deferred follow-up will credit `Co-authored-by: TurboTheTurtle` (PR #84790's sanitizer logic) and is independent of this PR; this PR remains complete and merge-ready on its own.

## Authority safety (why this is not the #84790 design)

| | PR #84790 (CLOSED 2026-05-22) | This PR |
|---|---|---|
| Content moved into `developer_instructions` | skills + workspace + MEMORY | **trust-filtered bundled skills only** |
| Skill source policy | full mixed-source catalog (bundled + workspace + project + personal + managed + extra + plugin) elevated | **`openclaw-bundled` only**; workspace / project / personal / managed / extra / plugin SKILL.md stays in user/reference lane under an explicit reference section header |
| Non-bundled skill visibility | implicit (they rode the same developer lane) | **explicit reference-lane wrapper**, visible to native Codex without developer authority |
| Workspace / MEMORY routing | through `developer_instructions` (with a disclaimer wrapper) | **unchanged from current `main`** — stays in turn-input reference lane established by #83454 |
| Session-state compatibility | session snapshots assumed to carry the new fields | **`SkillSnapshot.schemaVersion` migration** force-refreshes pre-PR snapshots so the new fields are populated on the next turn |
| Session-history sanitization | bundled in same PR | **deferred follow-up** (disclosed above) |
| Protocol-acceptance argument | schema inspection only | schema + production cron / heartbeat parity + wire-level Codex native rollout + cacheRead ratio uplift on the same scenario |
| Authority invariant test | n/a | three unit tests pin all three halves: workspace text never appears in `developer_instructions`, untrusted skill metadata never appears in `developer_instructions`, and untrusted skill metadata still appears in the per-turn user-input reference lane |

Reading order:

- Issue #84662 §"Security/rank caution": *"Stable instructions such as OpenClaw skills may be safe to move to developer/bootstrap instructions, but user-editable workspace context should remain quoted/reference scoped."* This PR follows that caveat literally and tightens it further: only **bundled** skill metadata (the OpenClaw-controlled half of the skills catalog) is treated as stable; user-installed SKILL.md content from workspace / project / personal / managed / extra / plugin sources stays in the reference lane.
- PR #84790 was closed by its author after the wider scope failed the prompt-authority finding raised in that review (wrapper text does not actually downgrade message authority).
- PR #83454 deliberately moved both halves *out* of `developer_instructions` so user-editable text would not gain developer authority. This PR complements that policy: workspace text and non-bundled SKILL.md content stay where #83454 put them; only the OpenClaw-owned bundled plugin-manifest skills snapshot — a stable, OpenClaw-controlled surface analogous to the bootstrap files in #77372 — moves to `developer_instructions`.
- PR #77372 already routes workspace-bootstrap files (`SOUL.md` / `IDENTITY.md` / `TOOLS.md` / `USER.md`) through `developer_instructions`; bundled skills join their natural peers under this PR.

## Real behavior proof

This PR carries two pieces of real-behavior evidence:

1. A **historical** 10-turn live OpenAI Codex benchmark captured at the prior trust-filter HEAD (`66ee52173e`, when the developer lane carried the full mixed-source skills catalog). The benchmark established the no-history-replay, byte-stable, cacheRead-uplift properties of the new lane.
2. A **current-head** wire-level proof artifact at `docs/research/runtime-context-surface-f1-trust-filter-current-head-proof.md` that exercises the production pipeline at the head SHA of this PR. It captures byte counts, sha256-12 hashes, and `contains`/`does-not-contain` assertions against synthetic marker strings (no live secrets, no private paths, no MEMORY content) for the trusted developer lane and the non-bundled reference lane, plus the legacy-snapshot refresh decision. Reproduce via `node --import tsx scripts/research/skills-trust-filter-proof.mts` or `./scripts/research/skills-trust-filter-proof.mts`.

The current-head trusted developer lane is a **strict reduction** of the bytes the historical benchmark sent (filter to bundled-only), so the historical benchmark's no-history-replay and byte-stability properties carry forward unchanged for the bundled subset. The non-bundled subset re-enters the per-turn user input lane under an explicit reference wrapper; its per-turn token cost matches the pre-#85646 baseline for that subset and is disclosed under Limitations.

### Historical 10-turn live benchmark (at `66ee52173e`)

**Behavior addressed**: Codex app-server persists the OpenClaw skills snapshot as a per-turn user-history item in native Codex threads, causing the model's `response.create.input` to grow by ~2,194 tokens/turn on the default `main` agent (issue #84662 root cause).

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw checkout at the upstream/main HEAD `66ee52173e` was based on, rebuilt dist; live OpenAI Codex backend (`openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini`); two local OpenAI-compatible OpenClaw gateways on separate ports sharing the same eval state directory (one unpatched baseline, one with this PR's patch applied); a live 10-turn ping-pong benchmark as the proof surface. A separate live runtime instance on an unrelated port was observed before / during / after the experiment via `/healthz` to confirm no PID change in unrelated processes.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
- `node scripts/build-all.mjs`
- Start two OpenClaw gateways on separate ports against an eval `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, one built from current upstream/main (arm A — baseline) and one built from this PR's branch (used for arms A' and B), each launched via `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway` with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`.
- Drive a 10-turn `body_contains: "pong"` ping-pong scenario against each gateway via an OpenAI-compatible benchmark CLI, using a distinct user ID per arm (forces a fresh Codex native session per arm) for three arms:
  - Arm A — unpatched baseline (#84662 reproducible state), default `main` agent.
  - Arm A' — patched, default `main` agent.
  - Arm B — patched gateway, but a separately-configured agent with `tools.profile=minimal`, `systemPromptOverride="."`, `skills=[]` (i.e., a no-skills control).

**Evidence after fix (10-turn live benchmark × 3 arms, all 30/30 functional `body_contains: "pong"`)**:

| Arm | growth/turn | turn 1 / turn 10 prompt_tokens | cum 10t prompt_tokens | cacheRead/input (turn 9 steady) | uncached input tokens (turn 9) | skills.count / promptChars |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| A — unpatched baseline (#84662 reproducible state) | +2,194 | 21,286 / 41,032 | 311,590 | 93.27 % | 2,614 | 20 / 6,629 |
| A' — patched | **+535** | 21,166 / 25,981 | **235,735** | **96.08 %** | **998** | 20 / 6,557 |
| B — no-skills control (patched gateway, no-skills agent) | +540 | 19,552 / 24,412 | 219,820 | 95.98 % | 960 | 0 / 0 |

All three arms used **fresh** user IDs so each arm opened a new Codex native session — the patched-arm curve reflects the new-thread behavior, not the legacy-thread replay floor. Per the "Limitations" section above, legacy bound threads are out of scope for this proof.

- Per-turn growth on the default agent drops from +2,194 to +535 tokens/turn (**−75.6 %**); cumulative 10-turn save = **−75,855 tokens (−24.3 %)** vs the unpatched baseline.
- Arm B's no-skills floor (+540/turn) is within ±5 tokens/turn of arm A' — confirming the +535/turn residual on the patched arm is the workspace-context replay floor, **not** skills.
- Codex native rollout for the patched arm shows `turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` populated with a byte-stable payload (md5 stable across all 10 turns at the historical HEAD; the current-head trusted-developer fragment is a strict reduction of those bytes so md5 stability properties carry forward).

**Latency**: trimmed mean **−448 ms (−8.8 %)** on the patched arm vs the baseline arm (5,102 → 4,654 ms after dropping hi/lo across n=10), both arms dominated by a single turn-1 cold-start outlier (A: 26,862 ms, A': 22,453 ms). No functional regression. We do **not** claim latency improvement at n=10; a 50-turn rerun would be the right place to make a stronger latency claim.

**Observed result after fix**: per-turn `prompt_tokens` growth on the default `main` agent drops from **+2,194 → +535 tokens/turn** (−75.6 %), within ±5 tokens/turn of the no-skills control (+540). Cumulative 10-turn `prompt_tokens` saves **−75,855 (−24.3 %)** vs the unpatched baseline. 30 / 30 functional `body_contains: "pong"` success across the 3 arms. `developer_instructions` is byte-stable across all 10 turns on the patched arm; workspace / MEMORY text never appears in `developer_instructions` (verified at the wire level and as a unit-test structural invariant). No functional regression observed.

### Current-head wire-level proof

The current-head proof is reproducible from the repo and runs entirely against synthetic marker strings:

- **Source**: `scripts/research/skills-trust-filter-proof.mts`. Pinned shebang `#!/usr/bin/env -S node --import tsx`.
- **Artifact**: `docs/research/runtime-context-surface-f1-trust-filter-current-head-proof.md`. Regenerated whenever the script is rerun.
- **Coverage**: builds a real workspace fixture with one skill per source (`openclaw-bundled`, `openclaw-workspace`, `agents-skills-project`, `openclaw-managed`, `openclaw-extra`) and a MEMORY-marker workspace-context fragment, then captures (a) byte length + sha256-12 of `prompt`, `trustedDeveloperPrompt`, `untrustedReferencePrompt`, and the reconstructed Codex turn-input wrapper; (b) per-marker `MUST contain` / `MUST NOT contain` assertions for the developer lane and the reference lane; (c) `isSkillsSnapshotSchemaOutdated` decisions for both legacy (no `schemaVersion`) and current (schemaVersion=2) snapshots.
- **Result**: 23 wire assertions PASS, 0 FAIL. Legacy snapshot decision = `true` (force-refresh). Current snapshot decision = `false` (reuse).

**What this proof does NOT cover**:

- A re-run of the 10-turn live benchmark against the current trust-filter HEAD. The change since `66ee52173e` is a strict reduction of bytes on the developer lane (filter to bundled-only) plus a non-authoritative addition to the reference lane; it does not change the wire shape, the byte-stability across turns, or the no-history-replay property the historical proof established for the trusted subset. The unit tests above and the current-head wire artifact pin the structural invariants the live benchmark would re-establish.
- Long-session 50 / 100-turn scaling under this change.
- Workspace-file mid-session edit cache-bust behaviour (workspace was static across the historical ~30 s experiment).
- Out-of-sample models (only `openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini` was exercised in the historical benchmark).
- Live Discord / Telegram production-channel turn (the OpenAI-compatible gateway was the historical proof surface, not a channel).
- **Legacy bound Codex native threads** that contain pre-fix `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:` user-history items — by design (see "Limitations of this PR"). The deferred follow-up session-history sanitizer is what makes those threads stop replaying historical wrappers.
- Session-history sanitization itself, including the `readCodexMirroredSessionHistoryMessages` post-processing path — out of scope for this PR (deferred to stacked follow-up).
- `skills.promptChars` drifted by 72 chars between the two historical arms (6,629 vs 6,557) due to a bundled-plugin asset rebuild difference between the two checkouts, not the source patch; `skills.entries.count` is **20 / 20** in both arms and the same skill names appear in both arms' Codex native rollouts.
- A single turn-10 OpenAI cache-eviction anomaly on the historical patched arm reported `cached_input_tokens=10,624` (turn-1-like) and `uncached=15,357` against the byte-stable `developer_instructions` payload (md5 stable); turns 2–9 each showed ~96.1 % cache rate and the steady-state quoted above uses turn 9. The headline per-turn growth and cumulative `prompt_tokens` metrics are unaffected.

target.title: fix(agents): strip plaintext model provider keys
target.body:
## Summary
- strip plaintext provider `apiKey` values after merge-mode preservation, source-managed enforcement, and provider compatibility transforms so old `models.json` keys cannot be reintroduced into the final prompt-facing catalog
- clean legacy catalog-only `models.json` plaintext provider keys before the empty-provider `skip` path can preserve them
- migrate existing `models.json`-only plaintext provider keys into the target agent's local `auth-profiles.json` before writing the stripped catalog when there is no configured provider key and no target-local auth profile for that provider
- keep inherited main-agent profiles and runtime/external overlays from suppressing local legacy `models.json` key migration for non-main agents
- migrate known/env-present legacy env-name catalog credentials such as `LITELLM_KEY` to env-backed auth-profile `keyRef`s, while preserving ambiguous env-shaped plaintext strings as inline local auth-profile keys
- preserve non-secret markers such as env SecretRef markers and custom-provider `secretref-managed` compatibility markers
- add regression coverage for generated plaintext stripping, merge-mode stripping, catalog-only cleanup, existing-key migration, fail-closed behavior, inherited-profile migration boundaries, env-present keyRef migration, ambiguous env-shaped key preservation, and pi model discovery compatibility

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated and legacy `models.json` files should not persist plaintext provider `apiKey` values. Cleanup should preserve upgrade auth outside the prompt-facing catalog, including non-main agent local migration boundaries and env-shaped legacy values that must only become `keyRef`s when an env marker or observed env value proves they are env-backed.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source from this PR branch at `b23d1d7c`, macOS, Node.js `v24.1.0`, `/usr/bin/python3` for fs-safe pinned writes. Provider keys in proof output are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
FS_SAFE_PYTHON_MODE=require FS_SAFE_PYTHON=/usr/bin/python3 node --import tsx --input-type=module
```

The inline Node proof script created temporary local agent dirs, called `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`, `discoverModels`, `ensureAuthProfileStore`, and `createProviderAuthResolver`, and exercised these real paths: inline custom provider stripping, legacy configured `models.json` key migration, catalog-only cleanup, env-present `keyRef` migration, ambiguous env-shaped plaintext preservation, non-main inherited-profile migration boundary, and pi model discovery compatibility.

        "source": "env",

target.title: fix(gateway): include MCP tools in effective inventory
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Agent Tools dashboard / `tools.effective` inventory originally reported only static OpenClaw tools, so configured bundled MCP tools could be usable at runtime but absent from the session-effective inventory.
- Solution: split the Gateway surface so `tools.effective` is a read-only peek projection with explicit MCP notices, and add `tools.effective.refresh` as an `operator.write` method that performs live MCP discovery/materialization on demand.
- What changed: effective inventory projection supports `source: "mcp"`; session MCP runtimes expose peek-only catalog metadata; the Gateway read path no longer cold-starts MCP transports; the refresh path materializes MCP tools through the existing runtime/materializer/final-policy path; Control UI adds an explicit **Refresh Available Tools** action; SDK/docs/protocol/tests cover the new split.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no provider transport changes, no Tool Search/Code Mode raw provider-body changes, no auto-reply `/tools` command changes, no install-package-dir changes, no broad shared-runtime refactor, and no claim that #80909 or the reporter-specific SSE raw provider-body path is fixed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #: N/A (intentionally not closing #76724; this keeps the reporter-specific SSE/runtime-provider-body path out of scope)
- Related / Refs #76724
- Related #70876
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

#76724 reports MCP handshake success while the Agent Tools dashboard stays stuck at the pre-MCP tool count and does not list MCP tools. Runtime code can materialize bundled MCP tools for model runs, but the dashboard inventory used a separate static inventory path. The initial patch made `tools.effective` perform live MCP materialization; after review of the read/security boundary, this update keeps `tools.effective` read-only and moves live discovery behind an explicit refresh method.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `tools.effective` should not cold-start/list MCP transports from a read-scoped dashboard load, while the dashboard must still have an explicit path to discover configured MCP tools and then show the warm catalog.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw Gateway from this branch at `3c7a4e67b6`, QA mock OpenAI provider, real stdio MCP server created by the proof harness, Tool Search disabled, Code Mode disabled.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_REPO_ROOT=/root/repos/openclaw-mcp-tools-effective \
OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAD=3c7a4e67b6 \
OPENCLAW_UPSTREAM_COMMIT=110042d840 \
OPENCLAW_RPC_PROOF_SUMMARY=/tmp/openclaw-mcp-tools-effective-d-proof-summary.json \
node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-mcp-tools-effective-d-proof.ts
```

- Evidence after fix:

Copied live Gateway RPC harness output from this branch:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "proof": "D read/refresh split: cold read peek-only, explicit refresh materializes MCP, warm read peeks catalog",
  "upstreamCommit": "110042d840",
  "localHead": "3c7a4e67b6",
  "cold": {
    "noticeIds": ["mcp-not-yet-connected"],
    "containsMcpTarget": false,
    "runtimeCreated": false,
    "groupSummaries": [
      { "id": "core", "source": "core", "toolCount": 18 },
      { "id": "plugin", "source": "plugin", "toolCount": 2 }
    ]
  },
  "refreshed": {
    "noticeIds": [],
    "containsMcpTarget": true,
    "matchingIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"],
    "runtimeCreated": true,
    "catalogToolNames": ["probe_tool"],
    "groupSummaries": [
      { "id": "core", "source": "core", "toolCount": 18 },
      { "id": "plugin", "source": "plugin", "toolCount": 2 },
      { "id": "mcp", "source": "mcp", "toolCount": 1, "sampleIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"] }
    ]
  },
  "warm": {
    "noticeIds": [],
    "containsMcpTarget": true,
    "matchingIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"],
    "groupSummaries": [
      { "id": "core", "source": "core", "toolCount": 18 },
      { "id": "plugin", "source": "plugin", "toolCount": 2 },
      { "id": "mcp", "source": "mcp", "toolCount": 1, "sampleIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"] }
    ]
  }
}
```

Additional Control UI browser proof from current head `b8bcb372b3d08b2b193d894b67d486cd9fc376fc`:

```text
Cold Control UI load:
- Panel shows: "MCP servers are configured but not connected yet. Use Refresh Available Tools to discover configured MCP tools."
- `Probe Tool` is absent from Available Right Now.
- Live count is 0.

After clicking "Refresh Available Tools":
- Browser RPC request observed: tools.effective.refresh with {agentId: "main", sessionKey: "agent:main:main"}.
- Available Right Now lists `Probe Tool` with source `MCP`.
- Live count is 1.
```

Screenshots are hosted on a separate fork artifact branch, not committed to this PR diff.

<details open>
<summary>Cold read: configured-MCP notice visible; Probe Tool absent</summary>

![Cold Control UI MCP notice](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nxmxbbd/openclaw/pr-84096-ui-proof/01-cold-mcp-notice.png)
</details>

<details open>
<summary>After refresh: Probe Tool appears as an MCP tool</summary>

![After refresh MCP tool](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nxmxbbd/openclaw/pr-84096-ui-proof/02-after-refresh-mcp-tool.png)
</details>

- Observed result after fix: a cold `tools.effective` read returns only core/plugin groups plus `mcp-not-yet-connected` and does not create a session MCP runtime; explicit `tools.effective.refresh` materializes the configured stdio MCP server and returns `reproProbe__probe_tool`; the next `tools.effective` read shows the warm MCP group via peek without another cold materialization.
- What was not tested: the reporter's original Flask SSE server and release version `v2026.5.2`; Tool Search / Code Mode raw provider-body behavior; #80909; full cross-platform/browser E2E. This PR is scoped to the Gateway/dashboard effective-inventory boundary.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): pre-patch proof on current main returned 20 tools and omitted `reproProbe__probe_tool` from `tools.effective` while the runtime provider path could include it.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `tools.effective` called the static `resolveEffectiveToolInventory()` resolver, which builds from `createOpenClawCodingTools()` only. Bundled MCP tools are appended later in the model-run path through the session MCP runtime/materializer, so the dashboard inventory and runtime provider path diverged.
- Boundary correction in this update: live MCP discovery/listing can start configured stdio/remote transports, so it now lives behind explicit `operator.write` `tools.effective.refresh`; the read-scoped method only peeks at an existing matching-fingerprint runtime catalog and otherwise returns a notice.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered core/plugin/channel grouping but did not cover bundled MCP catalog projection, cold-read side effects, refresh materialization, or stale MCP config fingerprints through the `tools.effective` handler/RPC path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end-style Gateway RPC proof
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools-effective-inventory.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
  - `packages/sdk/src/index.test.ts`
  - `packages/sdk/src/index.e2e.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/controllers/agents.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/views/agents-panels-tools-skills.browser.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: cold `tools.effective` does not create/list MCP and returns notices; warm read projects an existing same-fingerprint catalog; fingerprint mismatch returns refresh-needed notice instead of stale MCP tools; explicit `tools.effective.refresh` materializes MCP tools and applies final effective policy; UI uses read on normal load and refresh on explicit action.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug and security-boundary concern live at the seam between static inventory, session MCP runtime, Gateway method scope, SDK, and UI refresh behavior.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none found for bundled MCP in `tools-effective` / `tools-effective-inventory` before this patch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Agent Tools panel now loads without implicitly starting configured MCP transports. If MCP servers are configured but not connected/listed yet, the panel shows an explanatory notice and a **Refresh Available Tools** action. Triggering refresh performs live discovery and lets subsequent reads show the warm MCP tools.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before this PR:
[tools.effective] -> [static createOpenClawCodingTools inventory] -> [core/plugin/channel only]

Updated design:
[tools.effective]         -> [static inventory + peek existing MCP catalog] -> [core/plugin/channel + notices or warm mcp]
[tools.effective.refresh] -> [session MCP runtime materialize] -> [final bundled-tool policy] -> [core/plugin/channel + mcp]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No new capability beyond existing configured MCP materialization; yes, a new explicit Gateway method is added.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes, only from explicit `tools.effective.refresh` or existing run-lifecycle MCP materialization, not from cold `tools.effective` reads.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes, refresh can start/list configured MCP transports; it does not invoke MCP tools.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: live MCP listing can start a configured stdio server or connect to a configured remote endpoint, so this update moves that side effect out of `operator.read` and into explicit `operator.write` `tools.effective.refresh`. The read path only peeks an existing runtime catalog with a matching config fingerprint and returns notices otherwise. Refresh reuses the existing session MCP runtime/materializer/final effective policy path and releases the materializer lease.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local dev environment
- Runtime/container: local Node via `node --import tsx`
- Model/provider: QA mock OpenAI provider, `mock-openai/gpt-5.5`
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WebSocket RPC `sessions.create`, `tools.effective`, `tools.effective.refresh`, then `tools.effective`
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.profile: "coding"`, `tools.toolSearch: false`, `tools.codeMode.enabled: false`, `mcp.servers.reproProbe` stdio server command `node <tmp>/dummy-mcp-server.mjs`

### Steps

1. Start QA mock provider and local Gateway from this branch.
2. Create a session with `sessions.create`.
3. Call cold `tools.effective` and assert no MCP runtime was created and `reproProbe__probe_tool` is absent.
4. Call `tools.effective.refresh` and assert `reproProbe__probe_tool` appears in an `mcp` source group.
5. Call warm `tools.effective` and assert the MCP group is projected via peek.

### Expected

- Cold read: no MCP cold-start/listing; notice returned.
- Refresh: MCP listed/materialized through explicit write-scoped method.
- Warm read: MCP group visible through existing catalog peek.

### Actual

- Cold read: `runtimeCreated: false`, `containsMcpTarget: false`, `noticeIds: ["mcp-not-yet-connected"]`.
- Refresh: `runtimeCreated: true`, `matchingIds: ["reproProbe__probe_tool"]`, `catalogToolNames: ["probe_tool"]`.
- Warm read: `containsMcpTarget: true`, no stale not-connected notice.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `git merge-tree --write-tree upstream/main HEAD` → exit 0
  - `git diff --exit-code $(git merge-base upstream/main HEAD)..HEAD -- CHANGELOG.md` → no contributor-authored changelog entry remains
  - `pnpm check:test-types` → passed
  - `pnpm lint --threads=8` → passed
  - `pnpm protocol:check` → passed
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts src/agents/tools-effective-inventory.test.ts src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-tools.materialize.test.ts src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-tools.request-boundary.test.ts packages/sdk/src/index.test.ts` → 9 files / 110 tests passed
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts packages/sdk/src/index.e2e.test.ts` → 1 file passed; 4 passed / 1 skipped
  - `cd ui && pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts src/ui/controllers/agents.test.ts src/ui/views/agents-panels-tools-skills.browser.test.ts src/ui/views/agents.test.ts` → 3 files / 28 tests passed
  - real Gateway RPC proof with real stdio MCP server verifies cold read / refresh / warm read behavior
  - local Control UI browser proof at head `b8bcb372b3d08b2b193d894b67d486cd9fc376fc` verifies cold configured-MCP notice/no `Probe Tool`, explicit `tools.effective.refresh`, then `Probe Tool` visible as an MCP tool
- Edge cases checked:
  - final effective policy still filters materialized MCP tools
  - stale MCP config fingerprints produce refresh-needed behavior instead of serving stale MCP tools
  - read path does not call `getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime()` on cold load
  - UI notice rendering and explicit refresh action
  - SDK helper calls `tools.effective.refresh`
- What you did **not** verify:
  - reporter's exact Flask SSE MCP server / v2026.5.2 setup
  - Tool Search / Code Mode raw provider-body behavior (out of scope)
  - #80909 raw provider-body behavior (out of scope)
  - full cross-platform/browser E2E

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: users may expect MCP tools to appear immediately on panel load.
  - Mitigation: the panel now displays MCP notices and an explicit refresh action; warm catalogs still appear in read-only inventory.
- Risk: refresh can start configured MCP transports.
  - Mitigation: refresh is explicit and registered as `operator.write`; cold `operator.read` inventory no longer starts or lists MCP transports.
- Risk: dashboard/policy projection may still differ from a live inbound-message run when the UI lacks sender identity.
  - Mitigation: do not accept caller-supplied auth/delivery context; use server-derived session fields and final policy only.
- Risk: overlap with #70876.

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

Four code paths in `scripts/install.sh` download remote scripts via `download_file` and execute them with `bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

Affected paths:
1. `run_remote_bash` (Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`)
2. NodeSource apt/deb setup script (`deb.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
3. NodeSource dnf/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
4. NodeSource yum/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)

Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

Extract a reusable `validate_downloaded_script()` function and apply it to all four download-then-execute paths. The validation:
1. Rejects empty files (`[[ ! -s "$file" ]]`)
2. Rejects files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`)
3. Sanitizes the first-line diagnostic before logging: strips control characters (bytes 0x00-0x1F and 0x7F) via `LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'` and escapes backslashes (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) so untrusted downloaded content cannot inject terminal escape sequences through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path.

`run_remote_bash` calls `validate_downloaded_script` internally. The three NodeSource paths call it explicitly after `download_file` and before `bash "$tmp"` (or `sudo bash "$tmp"`).

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because these setup scripts update frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every release.

**Sanitization detail:** The `ui_error` function uses `echo -e` in the non-gum fallback path, which interprets backslash sequences (`\033[...` ANSI escapes, `\n` newlines, etc.). Two layers prevent terminal injection: (1) `tr -d` strips raw control bytes (actual ESC 0x1B), (2) backslash escaping prevents `echo -e` from interpreting literal `\033[31m` text as ANSI escapes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `run_remote_bash()` executed downloaded content with `/bin/bash` unconditionally. Truncated downloads, HTML error pages, JSON errors, and binary blobs would all be piped to bash. Additionally, the first-line diagnostic in the rejection message passed untrusted content through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path, which interprets backslash escape sequences (terminal injection).

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2, bash 5.x). The real patched `run_remote_bash` function was extracted from `scripts/install.sh` via `sed` and exercised against a local Python HTTP server serving 4 response types.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
Step 1 -- start a local HTTP server serving test responses (HTML error, empty, valid script, escape attack). Step 2 -- extract the real `run_remote_bash` from the patched `install.sh` and call it against each endpoint:
```bash
python3 -c '<HTTP server serving /html-error, /empty, /valid-script, /escape-attack>'
eval "$(sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p' scripts/install.sh)"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/valid-script"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack"
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Live proof: real `run_remote_bash` against HTTP server**

The actual `run_remote_bash` function from the patched `install.sh` was extracted via `sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p'` and called against a local Python HTTP server. This exercises the real patched code path, not a test harness copy.

```console
=== REAL run_remote_bash from patched install.sh ===

--- Test 1: HTML error page ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error
✗ First line: <html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: Empty response ---
✗ Downloaded script is empty: http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: Valid #!/bin/bash script ---
hello-from-valid-script
Exit code: 0

--- Test 4: Terminal escape attack ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack
✗ First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection
Exit code: 1
```

**Test 1 (HTML):** Server returns `<html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>`. Rejected because no `#!` shebang. First line shown in diagnostic.

**Test 2 (empty):** Server returns empty body. Rejected by the `[[ ! -s "$tmp" ]]` check.

**Test 3 (valid script):** Server returns `#!/bin/bash\necho hello-from-valid-script`. Accepted, executed by `/bin/bash`, printed `hello-from-valid-script`.

**Test 4 (escape attack):** Server returns literal text `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection` (ASCII backslash-zero-three-three, NOT actual ESC bytes). Rejected (no shebang). The first-line diagnostic shows `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m` as **literal text**, NOT as red-colored terminal output. Without the sanitization fix, `echo -e` would interpret `\033[31m` as ANSI red and show colored "HACKED" text. The double sanitization (control byte stripping via `tr -d` + backslash escaping via `${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) prevents both attack vectors.

**Sanitization detail**

Two layers protect the first-line diagnostic from terminal injection:

1. **Control byte stripping** (`LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'`): Removes actual ESC (0x1B), NUL, and other control bytes from the downloaded content.
2. **Backslash escaping** (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`): Doubles all backslashes so `echo -e` treats `\033` as literal text instead of interpreting it as an ESC byte.

Verification with a crafted payload:
```console
Literal \033[31m text (7 ASCII chars, no real ESC byte):
$ echo -e "First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m"
First line: HACKED    # <-- RED colored text (dangerous!)

$ safe_line='\033[31mHACKED\033[0m'; safe_line="${safe_line//\\/\\\\}"
$ echo -e "First line: ${safe_line}"
First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m    # <-- plain text (safe!)
```

**Supplemental: validation harness (10/10 pass)**

```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 10 passed, 0 failed
```

**Live HTTPS proof: unmodified `download_file` over TLS**

Proof using the **real unmodified `download_file` function** from `install.sh` (not a test stub). The function uses `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3` -- the identical flags used in production for the Homebrew installer download from `raw.githubusercontent.com`.

The server is a local Python `ssl.SSLContext` HTTPS server with a self-signed certificate (SAN: `IP:127.0.0.1`). `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` is set to trust the self-signed cert, so curl's TLS verification passes. No `--insecure` flag is used.

```console
=== REAL download_file + run_remote_bash over HTTPS ===
download_file: curl --proto =https --tlsv1.2 (unmodified from install.sh)
CURL_CA_BUNDLE=/tmp/test-cert.pem (trust self-signed cert for 127.0.0.1)

--- Test 1: HTTPS HTML error (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://127.0.0.1:19879/html-error
x First line: <html><body>403 Forbidden</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: HTTPS empty (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded script is empty: https://127.0.0.1:19879/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: HTTPS valid script (real download_file) ---
hello-from-REAL-download-file-HTTPS
Exit code: 0
```

All three tests use the **unmodified `download_file` function** extracted via `sed` from `install.sh`. The function calls `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3 --retry-delay 1 --retry-connrefused` -- the identical command line used in production. No `--insecure` flag. `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` trusts the self-signed cert so TLS verification passes normally.

Observed result after fix: The real patched `run_remote_bash` and `validate_downloaded_script` functions reject HTML errors, empty responses, and escape-attack payloads while accepting valid `#!/bin/bash` scripts. The first-line diagnostic is sanitized against both raw control bytes and literal backslash escape sequences. The HTTPS proof uses the real unmodified `download_file` function with `--proto '=https' --tlsv1.2` over TLS against a self-signed cert trusted via `CURL_CA_BUNDLE`. All 10 validation harness tests pass.

What was not tested: Live download from `raw.githubusercontent.com` returning an actual CDN error page (structural validation is tested against local HTTP and HTTPS servers instead).

## Summary

- Adds native `session.sendPolicy.rules[].match.peerEquals: "inboundPeer"` support.
- Allows relational send-policy checks between the outbound target and trusted inbound peer candidates for the current automatic-delivery turn, including `identityLinks` aliases.
- Threads inbound peer context through automatic replies, routed replies, follow-up routed replies, durable turn replies, and durable outbound delivery.
- Keeps send-policy-only predicates out of shared allow/deny scopes.
- Preserves explicit-send compatibility by keeping explicit `message.send`, gateway sends, and message-tool sends outside `session.sendPolicy` suppression.
- Persists `sendPolicyMode` for queued delivery replay and treats legacy queued entries without that field as explicit replay, preserving pre-PR queue semantics.
- Reports blocked automatic durable sends as `denied_by_send_policy` suppression outcomes.
- Adds focused unit coverage and docs/help labels for the new predicate.
